posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Just got in a 6-pack of these. Had a few others as well, but never had the courage to pop such a young bottle of BAMA. Had heard rumors about how good this young wine was, figured I had to go ahead and sacrifice a bottle.

And it is gorgeous.

But get this, 48 hours later.

Popped and poured on Friday night. Baby was tight. All was not right. Stuck it back in the fridge, went out to dinner last night. So forgot about it. Circle back this evening to enjoy with some Wagyu beef stew.

Did I say this wine is gorgeous?

I will admit that that the aromatics are a bit muted at first at this youthful stage, but it has so many hints of sublime classic claret. Old barn plank. Saddle leather. Cassis. And that scent of Bordeaux funk that you know it when you sniff it. It is the palate that is magnificent. Seamless, earth and fruit so wonderfully interwoven. Runs the spectrum from citrus reds to darker fruits. Lots of earthy elements in play, dry ground cover, mushrooms, and tea leaves. Some meaty broth as well. The finish has such intensity of deep, wild brambly red fruits, Margaux berries, with a salinity note that is intoxicating. The tannins are chalky and sweet.

This is a gorgeous young wine.

(94+ pts.)

IMG_04443024×4032 2.52 MB

posts.name: Nico P.
posts.cooked:
Thanks for this note. This wine really needs air time at this stage. My wine gulping group had a blind tasting of red BDX blends last October (photo of line up below). This wine was not decanted, just opened about four hours before the tasting. This wine was my favorite, though everyone else rated the others as their No. 1 (albeit no one rated the BAMA at the bottom).

When I retasted a 200ml sample set aside from that day (in a 200ml glass resealable bottle filled to minimize ullage then stored in a wine ref) sometime November, my impression was of a solidly structured light mouth feel wine with very nice aromatics.

Oct BDX Tasting1920×1440 317 KB

In another thread on this forum it was stated by William Kelley that 2012 was the last vintage produced, that according to Neal Martin (via Julian Marshall) there was some 2015 made but never released. The sentiment was to buy what you can. Your note on this wine prompted me to act on a long delayed plan to order more of this wine, so I ordered a case.

On a side note, I recently had the 2017 Ferriere (maybe some of the BAMA juice was in it?) and it was already good drinking PnP.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Nico_P:

On a side note, I recently had the 2017 Ferriere (maybe some of the BAMA juice was in it?) and it was already good drinking PnP.

I’m a big fan of Chateau Ferriere, been buying it for a long time, but admittedly, up until the 2016 vintage, it was rather hard to find in the US. It was, until the 2012 acquisition of BAMA vines, I think the smallest of the Classified Growths. I love many prior vintages, but I do think with the addition of the very old vine fruit from BAMA - some vines supposedly pre-phylloxera - and some excellent recent vintages, Ferriere is on a major role. I doubled up on 16 and 19 after trying them, both excellent. I have not tried 2017. Looks like 2020 gets some good buzz. I would not hesitate if I was looking for new releases.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
RyanC:

Great note. I opened the 98 a couple days ago. A delicious and singular wine in a lovely place on the maturity curve.

I’m dumb dumb. In the post above I neglected to mention the 1998. It’s become one of my faves in this great run of wines by BAMA. Glad you grabbed some. Didn’t really buy many left banks from this vintage, but continue to be amazed by how excellent the right banks are. Just got in today some more 98 St Ems.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Got my hands on one to try

2010 Château Bel Air-Marquis d'Aligre - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Margaux (2/14/2024)
Lovely nose with dusty tobacco, leather, dried leaves, newly sawed maple, hint of bell pepper; palate is reserved, brooding, continues some of the tobacco character, crushed rocks, fine but present tannins, acidity buoys the finish, an unusually restrained 2010. Drinkable now, but I think promises much more in a decade or three. (91 points)

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I have been politely corrected by a private email. It turns out that Chateau Ferriere did in fact buy BAMA but did not rehabilitate the vines. Apparently the BAMA vineyards had been subjected to heavy herbicides since the 1970s and about 25% of the vines were missing. So I guess that means they got the land but are starting over.

posts.name: Nico P.
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

I have been politely corrected by a private email. It turns out that Chateau Ferriere did in fact buy BAMA but did not rehabilitate the vines. Apparently the BAMA vineyards had been subjected to heavy herbicides since the 1970s and about 25% of the vines were missing. So I guess that means they got the land but are starting over.

Interesting. It appears some distributors & retailers were under the impression (perhaps mistaken) that the vineyards were managed according to organic principles (even if not certified), which are inconsistent with herbicide use. For example:

chambersstwines.com

Ch. Bel Air - Marquis d'Aligre, Margaux! A Nineteenth Century Wine in...

The wine spends a short time in wood...

stating “While not certified organic, there are no modern treatments and only a bit of organic compost as fertilizer“

Or:

rawwine.com

Chateau Bel-Air Marquis d'Aligre, Margaux

Explore the world of natural wine with the RAW WINE drinks directory. If you’re based in the UK, a curated collection of wines are also for sale.

Price: GBP 0.0

which claims “organic / biodynamic (uncertified)”
